story:
The child doesn’t speak at first. They just laugh and wave as their father stands on the platform, already seeming smaller with every jolt of the departing train. The train rattles; an announcement crackles, then fades. The father lifts a hesitant hand, a half-wave. His eyes smile, but they’re tired, a little sad. When the platform disappears, the child squirms in the seat across from the mother, clutching the new plushie their father pressed into their arms.

“I spy with my little eye,” the child sings, “something blue.”

“The jacket?” the mother guesses.

“No.”

“The seats?”

“Nope.”

A hint: “It’s everywhere, all the time.” The mother looks up. The sky, pale and wide. “The sky,” she says. The child nods. “It looks like Daddy’s eyes today.” The mother’s fingers tense around her cardigan button. “Funny how the sky seems more like him when we’re traveling.”

They play again. “I spy something red.” A hat? Shoes? The mother’s gaze snags on a deep red suitcase in the aisle. The one at home sits in the hallway, stranded by indecision. “That suitcase?” The child nods. “It looks like yours.” The mother laughs softly, throat tight.

“I spy something important,” the child says. The mother glances at a young man reading a thick book, and thinks of the folder buried in her bag—divorce papers smoothed flat on the table last night. Important. Unavoidable. “His book?” The child shakes their head and points. “The bookmark. That’s the important part.” The mother studies it. “Because people won’t forget where they paused?” The child tilts their head. “Because it means their story isn’t over yet.”

Silence threatens; the train keeps moving.

“I spy something untied.” The mother stalls with wrong guesses, then follows the child’s eyes to a pair of loose shoelaces, one trailing. “Why shoelaces?” she asks, leaning down to knot them. “Because they were together, but now they’re not,” the child says. The mother exhales. “Separated, but still part of the same pair.” The child beams. “They still. Belong. Together.” The mother nods. “I see.”

Fields replace the city. The child fogs the window with their breath. “I spy something white.” “The cloud?” “Guess again.” Rows of near-identical houses streak past. “The houses.” The child nods. “They look the same, but they’re all different inside, right?” The mother watches windows blur—lace in one, heavy curtains in another, a TV glow in a third. “Why do people make their houses match if they’re different inside?” the child asks. “Sometimes people want things to look the same. Even when they aren’t,” the mother says. “Like how you and Dad still eat dinner together?” the child adds. The mother pauses. “Sort of.”

“I spy something broken.” The mother’s heart stutters. Her reflection in the window wavers—tired eyes, tight mouth. The child points instead to a cracked station windowpane as they pass. A web of fractures holds it together. “Why don’t people fix certain things? And who decides?” The mother opens her mouth, then closes it. The train keeps going.

“I spy something invisible,” the child murmurs. “How can I guess what I can’t see?” the mother says, half-laughing. The child hugs the plushie and seems older for a moment. “It’s Dad.” The mother inhales, words sticking. The child turns back to the glass. “He’s not here, true. But it doesn’t mean he’s not with us. Just because something is invisible doesn’t mean it doesn’t exist.”

The mother tucks a curl behind the child’s ear, eyes stinging. The train hums.

“I spy something far away,” the child says. The mother looks at the pale blue sky stretching and stretching. “Me too, sweetheart. Me too.”
